<a name="arch-authentication-architecture-in-spa"></a>

### Authentication architecture in SPA

- BFF pattern — HttpOnly refresh cookie
- Access token in memory (short TTL)
- PKCE for OAuth public clients
- CSRF protection on cookie auth
- Never store refresh in localStorage
- Silent refresh + redirect on 401

**Production**

**Production example — Amazon**
*Context:* E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Authentication architecture in SPA..

*Why they chose this approach:* Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.
- **Pros:** Battle-tested idempotency patterns
- **Pros:** Massive A/B infra
- **Cons:** Legacy Webpack islands remain
- **Cons:** Many teams = inconsistent patterns

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **Trade-off accepted?** State what you sacrificed (time-to-market, consistency, bundle size) and why the requirement forced it.
- **Defer to v2?** Name one feature explicitly out of scope — offline, i18n, admin — to show prioritization discipline.
- **Proof metrics?** Error rate, p95 latency, deploy frequency, CWV p75, support ticket volume — tie arch to observable outcomes.

<a name="arch-cors-explain-preflight-and-fixes"></a>

### CORS — explain preflight and fixes

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**1. Cross-origin requests trigger OPTIONS preflight for non-simple requests.
2. Server returns Access-Control-Allow-Origin, Methods, Headers.
3. Fix: configure API CORS or same-origin proxy in dev (Vite proxy).

*Pro move:* tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Cross-origin requests trigger OPTIONS preflight for non-simple requests. Server returns Access-Control-Allow-Origin, Methods, Headers. Fix: configure API CORS or same-origin proxy in dev (Vite proxy).

**Production**

**Production example — Instagram**
*Context:* Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: CORS — explain preflight and fixes..

*Why they chose this approach:* Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.
- **Pros:** Feed scroll 60fps target
- **Pros:** Strong media CDN pipeline
- **Cons:** Hydration cost on web
- **Cons:** Moderation + ads complicate feed logic

<a name="arch-web-vitals-lcp-inp-cls-targets-and-fixes"></a>

### Web Vitals — LCP, INP, CLS targets and fixes

- **LCP <2.5s:** optimize hero image, SSR, CDN
- **INP <200ms:** break up JS, web workers, defer third-party
- **CLS <0.1:** size images, no layout shift ads

**Production**

**Production example — Netflix**
*Context:* Streaming UI prioritizes playback quality and browse LCP — perf budgets enforced per route. Relevant when discussing: Web Vitals — LCP, INP, CLS targets and fixes..

*Why they chose this approach:* Measure CrUX p75 before shipping player or homepage changes.
- **Pros:** Strong RUM culture
- **Pros:** Edge CDN for static assets
- **Cons:** Personalization complicates caching
- **Cons:** DRM constraints on web player

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**1. Tokens (color, spacing, typography) → primitives (Button, Input) → patterns (LoginForm).
2. Storybook + visual regression (Chromatic).
3. Semantic tokens for theming.

*Pro move:* tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Tokens (color, spacing, typography) → primitives (Button, Input) → patterns (LoginForm). Storybook + visual regression (Chromatic). Semantic tokens for theming.

**Production**

**Production example — Flipkart**
*Context:* Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: Design system — tokens, components, documentation..

*Why they chose this approach:* Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.
- **Pros:** Mobile web highly optimized
- **Pros:** Strong sale-day runbooks
- **Cons:** Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
- **Cons:** MFE skew incidents during peaks

<a name="arch-feature-flags-architecture"></a>

### Feature flags architecture

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**1. LaunchDarkly / Unleash / custom.
2. Evaluate flags server-side when security-critical.
3. Client flags for UI experiments.
4. Avoid flag spaghetti — sunset flags.

*Pro move:* tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

LaunchDarkly / Unleash / custom. Evaluate flags server-side when security-critical. Client flags for UI experiments. Avoid flag spaghetti — sunset flags.

**Production**

**Production example — Amazon**
*Context:* E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Feature flags architecture..

*Why they chose this approach:* Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.
- **Pros:** Battle-tested idempotency patterns
- **Pros:** Massive A/B infra
- **Cons:** Legacy Webpack islands remain
- **Cons:** Many teams = inconsistent patterns

<a name="arch-error-handling-strategy-layers"></a>

### Error handling strategy — layers

- Boundary: React ErrorBoundary per route
- API: normalized error type, toast user message
- Global: Sentry capture with breadcrumbs
- Retry: exponential backoff idempotent GETs

**Production**

**Production example — Instagram**
*Context:* Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: Error handling strategy — layers..

*Why they chose this approach:* Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.
- **Pros:** Feed scroll 60fps target
- **Pros:** Strong media CDN pipeline
- **Cons:** Hydration cost on web
- **Cons:** Moderation + ads complicate feed logic

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **Trade-off accepted?** State what you sacrificed (time-to-market, consistency, bundle size) and why the requirement forced it.
- **Defer to v2?** Name one feature explicitly out of scope — offline, i18n, admin — to show prioritization discipline.
- **Proof metrics?** Error rate, p95 latency, deploy frequency, CWV p75, support ticket volume — tie arch to observable outcomes.

<a name="arch-testing-pyramid-for-frontend-at-scale"></a>

### Testing pyramid for frontend at scale

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**1. Many unit (utils, hooks, reducers) → integration (RTL + MSW) → fewer E2E (Playwright critical paths).
2. Visual regression for design system.
3. Contract tests for API.

*Pro move:* tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Many unit (utils, hooks, reducers) → integration (RTL + MSW) → fewer E2E (Playwright critical paths). Visual regression for design system. Contract tests for API.

**Production**

**Production example — Netflix**
*Context:* Streaming UI prioritizes playback quality and browse LCP — perf budgets enforced per route. Relevant when discussing: Testing pyramid for frontend at scale..

*Why they chose this approach:* Measure CrUX p75 before shipping player or homepage changes.
- **Pros:** Strong RUM culture
- **Pros:** Edge CDN for static assets
- **Cons:** Personalization complicates caching
- **Cons:** DRM constraints on web player

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **Trade-off accepted?** State what you sacrificed (time-to-market, consistency, bundle size) and why the requirement forced it.
- **Defer to v2?** Name one feature explicitly out of scope — offline, i18n, admin — to show prioritization discipline.

<a name="arch-ci-cd-pipeline-for-frontend"></a>

### CI/CD pipeline for frontend

```
# PR: lint → typecheck → unit → build → bundle size check
# main: + E2E staging → deploy preview → prod canary
```

Tools: GitHub Actions, Vercel/Netlify previews, semantic release.

**Production**

**Production example — Flipkart**
*Context:* Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: CI/CD pipeline for frontend..

*Why they chose this approach:* Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.
- **Pros:** Mobile web highly optimized
- **Pros:** Strong sale-day runbooks
- **Cons:** Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
- **Cons:** MFE skew incidents during peaks

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **Trade-off accepted?** State what you sacrificed (time-to-market, consistency, bundle size) and why the requirement forced it.
- **Defer to v2?** Name one feature explicitly out of scope — offline, i18n, admin — to show prioritization discipline.

<a name="arch-monorepo-tooling-nx-vs-turborepo"></a>

### Monorepo tooling — Nx vs Turborepo

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**1. Both: task caching, affected builds.
2. Nx: generators, module graph, enforced boundaries.
3. Turborepo: simpler, pipeline focused.
4. Share design-system, configs across apps.

*Pro move:* tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Both: task caching, affected builds. Nx: generators, module graph, enforced boundaries. Turborepo: simpler, pipeline focused. Share design-system, configs across apps.

**Production**

**Production example — Amazon**
*Context:* E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Monorepo tooling — Nx vs Turborepo..

*Why they chose this approach:* Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.
- **Pros:** Battle-tested idempotency patterns
- **Pros:** Massive A/B infra
- **Cons:** Legacy Webpack islands remain
- **Cons:** Many teams = inconsistent patterns

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **Trade-off accepted?** State what you sacrificed (time-to-market, consistency, bundle size) and why the requirement forced it.
- **Defer to v2?** Name one feature explicitly out of scope — offline, i18n, admin — to show prioritization discipline.
- **Proof metrics?** Error rate, p95 latency, deploy frequency, CWV p75, support ticket volume — tie arch to observable outcomes.

<a name="arch-ssr-vs-ssg-vs-isr-vs-csr-choose-per-page"></a>

### SSR vs SSG vs ISR vs CSR — choose per page

- **SSG:** marketing, docs — build time HTML
- **ISR:** semi-static e-commerce listings
- **SSR:** personalized, SEO dynamic
- **CSR:** dashboards behind auth

**Production**

**Production example — Myntra**
*Context:* Category PLP ISR revalidate 60s — sale hour drops to on-demand revalidate.

*Why they chose this approach:* Edge SSR for SEO PLP; CSR for account settings.
- **Pros:** Google indexes sale categories
- **Pros:** CDN serves stale while revalidating
- **Cons:** Cache invalidation complexity on price drop
- **Cons:** SSR cost at peak traffic

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **Trade-off accepted?** State what you sacrificed (time-to-market, consistency, bundle size) and why the requirement forced it.

<a name="arch-real-time-architecture-websockets-vs-sse-vs-polling"></a>

### Real-time architecture — WebSockets vs SSE vs polling

- **WebSocket:** bidirectional — chat, live GPS
- **SSE:** server push one-way — notifications
- **Polling:** simple, higher latency — 30s fleet map OK for demo

**Reasoning**

**Reasoning** Custom events or message bus decouple modules — MFE shell doesn't import remote internals.

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**
Custom events or message bus decouple modules — MFE shell doesn't import remote internals.

**Example**

bus.emit({ type: "CART_UPDATED", count })

**Trade-off** Global bus can become spaghetti — document event catalog.

**Reasoning**

**Reasoning** Client generates UUID once per user action — retry safe on network failure.

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**
Client generates UUID once per user action — retry safe on network failure.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

**Example**

**Example**
```
Idempotency-Key header on POST payment
```

**Tradeoff**

**Trade-off** Server must store key window 24h.

> **Tip:** Prevents double charge UX bug.

<a name="arch-optimistic-ui-409-rollback"></a>

**Reasoning**

**Reasoning** Version conflict from server — revert optimistic patch show merge UI.

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**
Version conflict from server — revert optimistic patch show merge UI.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

**Example**

**Example**

if (res.status === 409) rollback(); showConflictDialog(serverState)

> **Tip:** Collaborative editing needs CRDT not optimistic alone.

- **Cons:** Hydration cost on web

<a name="arch-cdn-caching-strategy"></a>

### CDN caching strategy

**Reasoning**

**Reasoning** Hashed assets immutable 1 year; index.html short cache or no-cache.

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**
Hashed assets immutable 1 year; index.html short cache or no-cache.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

**Example**

**Example**
```
Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable
```

**Tradeoff**

**Trade-off** CDN purge on deploy for non-hashed assets.

> **Tip:** CloudFront/Fastly interview names.

**Production**

**Production example — Flipkart**
*Context:* Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: CDN caching strategy.

*Why they chose this approach:* Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.
- **Pros:** Mobile web highly optimized
- **Pros:** Strong sale-day runbooks
- **Cons:** Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
- **Cons:** MFE skew incidents during peaks

**Example**
```
size-limit preset-app 250KB
```

**Tradeoff**

**Trade-off** Budget creep gradual without gate.

> **Tip:** Pair with analyzer artifact on CI.

**Production**

**Production example — Instagram**
*Context:* Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: Bundle budget CI gate.

*Why they chose this approach:* Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.
- **Pros:** Feed scroll 60fps target
- **Pros:** Strong media CDN pipeline
- **Cons:** Hydration cost on web
- **Cons:** Moderation + ads complicate feed logic

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **Trade-off accepted?** State what you sacrificed (time-to-market, consistency, bundle size) and why the requirement forced it.
- **Defer to v2?** Name one feature explicitly out of scope — offline, i18n, admin — to show prioritization discipline.
- **Proof metrics?** Error rate, p95 latency, deploy frequency, CWV p75, support ticket volume — tie arch to observable outcomes.

<a name="arch-graphql-vs-rest-frontend"></a>

### GraphQL vs REST frontend

**Reasoning**

**Reasoning** GraphQL: client normalized cache Apollo/Relay, flexible queries. REST: simpler caching HTTP semantics + TanStack Query.

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**1. GraphQL: client normalized cache Apollo/Relay, flexible queries.
2. REST: simpler caching HTTP semantics + TanStack Query.

*Pro move:* tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

**Example**

**Example**
```
Query colocation vs multiple REST endpoints
```

**Tradeoff**

**Trade-off** GraphQL N+1 server problem not FE but mention.

> **Tip:** Pick based on API team capabilities.

**Production**

**Production example — Amazon**
*Context:* E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: GraphQL vs REST frontend.

*Why they chose this approach:* Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.
- **Pros:** Battle-tested idempotency patterns
- **Pros:** Massive A/B infra
- **Cons:** Legacy Webpack islands remain
- **Cons:** Many teams = inconsistent patterns

## React Context API (22 questions) High priority

<a name="context-when-should-you-use-context-vs-props-vs-external-store"></a>

### When should you use Context vs props vs external store

- **Props:** default — explicit data flow, easy to trace.
- **Context:** deeply shared stable data (theme, auth, locale) consumed by many branches.
- **Store (Redux/Zustand):** frequent updates, complex mutations, middleware, DevTools.

> **Tip:** Context is not a performance optimization — it is a dependency injection mechanism.

**Production**

**Production example — Flipkart**
*Context:* ThemeContext + CartContext split — cart qty no longer re-renders entire PLP tree.

*Why they chose this approach:* Memoized value objects + split providers.
- **Pros:** Measurable render reduction
- **Pros:** Simple mental model vs Redux for cart only
- **Cons:** Still coarser than Zustand selectors
- **Cons:** Provider hell if over-split

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **Split context when?** When unrelated consumers re-render together — split by theme vs auth vs cart; or move fast-changing data to external store.
- **Debug re-renders?** React DevTools highlight updates, why-did-you-render, or split context and compare commit counts.
- **vs Zustand?** Zustand selectors subscribe to slices; Context needs memoized value + split providers to match that granularity.

**Probes**

**If the interviewer pushes deeper**- Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
- Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
- State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

---

<a name="context-why-does-a-context-provider-cause-all-consumers-to-re-render"></a>

### Why does a Context provider cause all consumers to re-render

**Reasoning**

**Reasoning** React compares context `value` by reference. New object every render → all `useContext` subscribers scheduled for re-render, even if they only read `theme` and you changed unrelated `cart`. This is the #1 Context performance footgun.

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**1. React compares context `value` by reference.
2. New object every render → all `useContext` subscribers scheduled for re-render, even if they only read `theme` and you changed unrelated `cart`.
3. This is the #1 Context performance footgun.

*Pro move:* tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

**Example**

**Bad vs good**

```
// BAD — new object every Parent render
function AppProvider({ children }) {
  const [user, setUser] = useState(null);
  return (
     {}, logout: () => {} }}>
      {children}
    
  );
}

// GOOD — stable reference until deps change
const login = useCallback(async (u, p) => { ... }, []);
const logout = useCallback(() => { ... }, []);
const value = useMemo(() => ({ user, login, logout }), [user, login, logout]);
```

**Tradeoff**

**Fix hierarchy** 1) memoize value 2) split contexts 3) selectors (use-context-selector / Zustand) 4) move fast-changing data to TanStack Query or external store.

**Production**

**Production example — Flipkart**
*Context:* ThemeContext + CartContext split — cart qty no longer re-renders entire PLP tree.

*Why they chose this approach:* Memoized value objects + split providers.
- **Pros:** Measurable render reduction
- **Pros:** Simple mental model vs Redux for cart only
- **Cons:** Still coarser than Zustand selectors
- **Cons:** Provider hell if over-split

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **Split context when?** When unrelated consumers re-render together — split by theme vs auth vs cart; or move fast-changing data to external store.
- **Debug re-renders?** React DevTools highlight updates, why-did-you-render, or split context and compare commit counts.
- **vs Zustand?** Zustand selectors subscribe to slices; Context needs memoized value + split providers to match that granularity.

**Probes**

**If the interviewer pushes deeper**- Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
- Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
- State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

<a name="context-split-context-pattern-state-vs-dispatch"></a>

### Split context pattern — state vs dispatch

```
const StateCtx = createContext(null);
const DispatchCtx = createContext(null);

function Provider({ children }) {
  const [state, dispatch] = useReducer(reducer, init);
  return (
    
      {children}
    
  );
}
// Components that only dispatch never re-render on state change
```

**Production**

**Production example — Flipkart**
*Context:* ThemeContext + CartContext split — cart qty no longer re-renders entire PLP tree.

*Why they chose this approach:* Memoized value objects + split providers.
- **Pros:** Measurable render reduction
- **Pros:** Simple mental model vs Redux for cart only
- **Cons:** Still coarser than Zustand selectors
- **Cons:** Provider hell if over-split

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **Split context when?** When unrelated consumers re-render together — split by theme vs auth vs cart; or move fast-changing data to external store.
- **Debug re-renders?** React DevTools highlight updates, why-did-you-render, or split context and compare commit counts.
- **vs Zustand?** Zustand selectors subscribe to slices; Context needs memoized value + split providers to match that granularity.

<a name="context-usecontextselector-pattern-without-external-library"></a>

### useContextSelector pattern without external library

```
function useAppSelector(selector) {
  const state = useContext(StateCtx);
  const selected = selector(state);
  const ref = useRef(selected);
  if (!Object.is(ref.current, selected)) ref.current = selected;
  return ref.current;
}
```

For production, prefer `use-context-selector` or Zustand with selectors — hand-rolled equality is error-prone.

**Production**

**Production example — Flipkart**
*Context:* ThemeContext + CartContext split — cart qty no longer re-renders entire PLP tree.

*Why they chose this approach:* Memoized value objects + split providers.
- **Pros:** Measurable render reduction
- **Pros:** Simple mental model vs Redux for cart only
- **Cons:** Still coarser than Zustand selectors
- **Cons:** Provider hell if over-split

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **Split context when?** When unrelated consumers re-render together — split by theme vs auth vs cart; or move fast-changing data to external store.
- **Debug re-renders?** React DevTools highlight updates, why-did-you-render, or split context and compare commit counts.
- **vs Zustand?** Zustand selectors subscribe to slices; Context needs memoized value + split providers to match that granularity.

**Probes**

**If the interviewer pushes deeper**- Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
- Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.

<a name="context-context-usereducer-vs-redux-for-fleetpanda-style-apps"></a>

### Context + useReducer vs Redux for FleetPanda-style apps

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**1. Context+reducer fits moderate shared state, single team, no time-travel needs.
2. Redux when: many slices, RTK Query, listener middleware, large team conventions.
3. FleetPanda uses Context because assignment scope is one SPA with versioned localStorage.

*Pro move:* tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Context+reducer fits moderate shared state, single team, no time-travel needs. Redux when: many slices, RTK Query, listener middleware, large team conventions. FleetPanda uses Context because assignment scope is one SPA with versioned localStorage.

- **Cons:** Provider hell if over-split

<a name="context-default-context-value-why-undefined-custom-hook-throw"></a>

### Default context value — why undefined + custom hook throw

```
const Ctx = createContext(undefined);
export function useCtx() {
  const v = useContext(Ctx);
  if (v === undefined) throw new Error('useCtx must be used within Provider');
  return v;
}
```

Catches missing provider at dev time instead of silent null bugs.

**Production**

**Production example — Flipkart**

<a name="context-context-vs-react-19-use-hook"></a>

### Context vs React 19 use() hook

```
// use() can read context conditionally (hooks rules exception)
function Row({ showMeta }) {
  if (showMeta) {
    const theme = use(ThemeContext); // conditional read
  }
}
```

`use()` also unwraps promises in Suspense boundaries — different use case from classic context.

**Production**

**Production example — Flipkart**

2. Mega context couples unrelated re-renders.

**Example**

ThemeProvider + CartProvider separate

**Reasoning** Large forms in context cause wide re-renders on every keystroke — prefer RHF local state or colocated state.

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**
Large forms in context cause wide re-renders on every keystroke — prefer RHF local state or colocated state.

**Example**
```
Wizard step context OK if only 3 fields shared across steps

**Trade-off** Context for every input field = perf smell.

> **Tip:** Use context for submit handler registration not field values.

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **Split context when?** When unrelated consumers re-render together — split by theme vs auth vs cart; or move fast-changing data to external store.
- **Debug re-renders?** React DevTools highlight updates, why-did-you-render, or split context and compare commit counts.
- **vs Zustand?** Zustand selectors subscribe to slices; Context needs memoized value + split providers to match that granularity.

**Probes**

**If the interviewer pushes deeper**- Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
- Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
- State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

<a name="context-colocate-provider-closest-to-consumers"></a>

### Colocate provider closest to consumers

**Reasoning**

**Reasoning** Not every app needs root AppProvider — nested providers reduce re-render scope.

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**
Not every app needs root AppProvider — nested providers reduce re-render scope.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

**Example**

**Example**
```
ModalProvider only wrapping modal subtree if possible
```

**Tradeoff**

**Trade-off** Multiple providers at root still OK if memoized.

> **Tip:** Architecture review question.

**Production**

**Production example — Flipkart**
*Context:* ThemeContext + CartContext split — cart qty no longer re-renders entire PLP tree.

*Why they chose this approach:* Memoized value objects + split providers.
- **Pros:** Measurable render reduction
- **Pros:** Simple mental model vs Redux for cart only
- **Cons:** Still coarser than Zustand selectors
- **Cons:** Provider hell if over-split

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **Split context when?** When unrelated consumers re-render together — split by theme vs auth vs cart; or move fast-changing data to external store.
- **Debug re-renders?** React DevTools highlight updates, why-did-you-render, or split context and compare commit counts.
- **vs Zustand?** Zustand selectors subscribe to slices; Context needs memoized value + split providers to match that granularity.

**Example**

Children prop pattern passes rendered subtree

> **Tip:** Don't context for data one branch needs.

**Reasoning**

## Browser Internals (32 questions) High priority

<a name="browser-explain-the-browser-rendering-pipeline-end-to-end"></a>

### Explain the browser rendering pipeline end-to-end

**Reasoning**

**Reasoning** After bytes arrive, rendering is: parse HTML → build DOM; parse CSS → CSSOM; combine into render tree (visible nodes only); layout (compute geometry); paint (rasterize); composite (GPU layers). JS can trigger additional layout/paint cycles. This connects directly to the URL-bar journey in E2E section.

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**1. After bytes arrive, rendering is: parse HTML → build DOM; parse CSS → CSSOM; combine into render tree (visible nodes only); layout (compute geometry); paint (rasterize); composite (GPU layers).
2. JS can trigger additional layout/paint cycles.
3. This connects directly to the URL-bar journey in E2E section.

*Pro move:* tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

**Example**

**Example**
```
HTML bytes → Tokenizer → DOM tree
CSS bytes → CSSOM
DOM + CSSOM → Render tree → Layout → Paint → Composite
JS execution can mutate DOM → repeat Layout/Paint if needed
```

**Tradeoff**

**Trade-off** Composite-only properties (transform, opacity) skip layout/paint when possible — key INP optimization.

> **Tip:** Draw this pipeline on whiteboard when asked "what happens when page loads".

**Production**

**Production example — Netflix**
*Context:* Streaming UI prioritizes playback quality and browse LCP — perf budgets enforced per route. Relevant when discussing: Explain the browser rendering pipeline end-to-end..

*Why they chose this approach:* Measure CrUX p75 before shipping player or homepage changes.
- **Pros:** Strong RUM culture
- **Pros:** Edge CDN for static assets
- **Cons:** Personalization complicates caching
- **Cons:** DRM constraints on web player

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **DevTools?** Name exact panel: Network waterfall, Performance trace, Application cache, Rendering paint flashing.
- **Core Web Vital?** Map answer to LCP (load), INP (input delay), CLS (layout shift) — pick the most relevant one.
- **Safari / WebView?** ITP cookie limits, missing APIs (e.g. some SW features), passive touch listeners — state what you polyfill or degrade.

**Probes**

**If the interviewer pushes deeper**- Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
- Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
- State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

<a name="browser-reflow-vs-repaint-vs-composite-what-triggers-each"></a>

### Reflow vs repaint vs composite — what triggers each

- **Reflow:** geometry change — width, font, DOM insert/remove.
- **Repaint:** visual only — color, visibility (no layout).
- **Composite:** transform, opacity — often GPU, skips layout/paint.

```
/* Prefer compositor-friendly */
.animate { transform: translateX(100px); opacity: 0.5; }
/* Avoid */ .bad { left: 100px; top: 50px; }
```

**Production**

**Production example — Flipkart**
*Context:* Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: Reflow vs repaint vs composite — what triggers each?.

*Why they chose this approach:* Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.
- **Pros:** Mobile web highly optimized
- **Pros:** Strong sale-day runbooks
- **Cons:** Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
- **Cons:** MFE skew incidents during peaks

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **DevTools?** Name exact panel: Network waterfall, Performance trace, Application cache, Rendering paint flashing.
- **Core Web Vital?** Map answer to LCP (load), INP (input delay), CLS (layout shift) — pick the most relevant one.
- **Safari / WebView?** ITP cookie limits, missing APIs (e.g. some SW features), passive touch listeners — state what you polyfill or degrade.

**Probes**

**If the interviewer pushes deeper**- Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
- Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
- State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

<a name="browser-event-loop-microtasks-vs-macrotasks-with-full-trace"></a>

### Event loop — microtasks vs macrotasks (with full trace)

**Reasoning**

**Reasoning** JS is single-threaded. The event loop drains the call stack, then runs all microtasks (promises, queueMicrotask), then one macrotask (setTimeout, I/O, UI event), then renders if needed. Understanding order explains "why setState in promise runs before setTimeout".

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**1. JS is single-threaded.
2. The event loop drains the call stack, then runs all microtasks (promises, queueMicrotask), then one macrotask (setTimeout, I/O, UI event), then renders if needed.
3. Understanding order explains "why setState in promise runs before setTimeout".

*Pro move:* tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

**Example**

**Classic interview trace**

```
console.log('1');
setTimeout(() => console.log('2'), 0);
Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log('3'));
queueMicrotask(() => console.log('4'));
console.log('5');
// Output: 1, 5, 3, 4, 2
```

**Real bug:** Measuring DOM in setTimeout(0) after React setState may run before React commit — use `useLayoutEffect` or `flushSync` when you must read post-update DOM.

**Tradeoff**

**INP connection** Long microtask chains block paint — break work with scheduler/yield or workers for >50ms tasks.

**Production**

**Production example — Instagram**
*Context:* Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: Event loop — microtasks vs macrotasks (with full trace)..

*Why they chose this approach:* Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.
- **Pros:** Feed scroll 60fps target
- **Pros:** Strong media CDN pipeline
- **Cons:** Hydration cost on web
- **Cons:** Moderation + ads complicate feed logic

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **DevTools?** Name exact panel: Network waterfall, Performance trace, Application cache, Rendering paint flashing.
- **Core Web Vital?** Map answer to LCP (load), INP (input delay), CLS (layout shift) — pick the most relevant one.
- **Safari / WebView?** ITP cookie limits, missing APIs (e.g. some SW features), passive touch listeners — state what you polyfill or degrade.

**Probes**

**If the interviewer pushes deeper**- Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
- Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
- State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

<a name="browser-how-many-threads-processes-in-modern-chrome-for-a-tab"></a>

### How many threads/processes in modern Chrome for a tab

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**1. Site-isolated renderer process per origin (generally).
2. GPU process, network service, browser process separate.
3. Multiple tabs same site may share process (with exceptions).
4. Explains memory vs security trade-off.

*Pro move:* tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Site-isolated renderer process per origin (generally). GPU process, network service, browser process separate. Multiple tabs same site may share process (with exceptions). Explains memory vs security trade-off.

**Production**

**Production example — Amazon**
*Context:* E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: How many threads/processes in modern Chrome for a tab?.

*Why they chose this approach:* Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.
- **Pros:** Battle-tested idempotency patterns
- **Pros:** Massive A/B infra
- **Cons:** Legacy Webpack islands remain
- **Cons:** Many teams = inconsistent patterns

<a name="browser-same-origin-policy-and-cors-browser-perspective"></a>

### Same-origin policy and CORS — browser perspective

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**1. Browser blocks reading cross-origin response unless server sends CORS headers.
2. Simple GET may skip preflight; JSON POST with custom headers triggers OPTIONS preflight.

*Pro move:* tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Browser blocks reading cross-origin response unless server sends CORS headers. Simple GET may skip preflight; JSON POST with custom headers triggers OPTIONS preflight.

```
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://app.example.com
Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true
```

**Production**

**Production example — Flipkart**
*Context:* Checkout BFF same-origin — browser never hits payment microservice CORS directly.

*Why they chose this approach:* BFF pattern for multi-backend ecommerce.
- **Pros:** Single CORS surface
- **Pros:** Hide internal service URLs
- **Cons:** BFF team bottleneck
- **Cons:** Extra latency hop

**Followup**

<a name="browser-cookie-attributes-secure-httponly-samesite"></a>

### Cookie attributes — Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite

```
Set-Cookie: session=abc; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax; Path=/; Max-Age=3600
```
- **HttpOnly:** no document.cookie access — XSS mitigation
- **SameSite=Strict/Lax:** CSRF mitigation
- **Secure:** HTTPS only

**Production**

**Production example — Flipkart**

<a name="browser-service-worker-lifecycle-and-caching-strategies"></a>

### Service Worker lifecycle and caching strategies

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**1. Install → wait → activate → fetch intercept.
2. Strategies:

*Pro move:* tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Install → wait → activate → fetch intercept. Strategies:
- **Cache-first:** static assets
- **Network-first:** API data
- **Stale-while-revalidate:** fonts, avatars

```
self.addEventListener('fetch', (e) => {
  e.respondWith(caches.match(e.request).then(c => c || fetch(e.request)));
});
```

**Production**

**Production example — Myntra**
*Context:* PWA SW caches app shell; product API network-first during End of Reason Sale.

*Why they chose this approach:* Versioned precache + runtime image cache with TTL.
- **Pros:** Repeat visit TTI faster
- **Pros:** Offline recently viewed PLP
- **Cons:** Stale price if cache-first on API
- **Cons:** SW update UX needed

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **DevTools?** Name exact panel: Network waterfall, Performance trace, Application cache, Rendering paint flashing.
- **Core Web Vital?** Map answer to LCP (load), INP (input delay), CLS (layout shift) — pick the most relevant one.
- **Safari / WebView?** ITP cookie limits, missing APIs (e.g. some SW features), passive touch listeners — state what you polyfill or degrade.

<a name="browser-http-1-1-vs-http-2-vs-http-3-for-frontend-perf"></a>

### HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2 vs HTTP/3 for frontend perf

- **H1:** 6 conn limit per domain — bundling mattered
- **H2:** multiplexing — smaller chunks OK
- **H3:** QUIC over UDP — faster on lossy networks

Still minimize JS; protocol doesn't fix large bundles.

**Production**

**Production example — Netflix**
*Context:* Streaming UI prioritizes playback quality and browse LCP — perf budgets enforced per route. Relevant when discussing: HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2 vs HTTP/3 for frontend perf..

- **Pros:** Strong RUM culture
- **Pros:** Edge CDN for static assets
- **Cons:** Personalization complicates caching
- **Cons:** DRM constraints on web player

<a name="browser-memory-leaks-in-spas-common-causes"></a>

### Memory leaks in SPAs — common causes

- Intervals/listeners without cleanup
- Detached DOM nodes held in closures
- Global caches growing unbounded
- WebSocket subscriptions
- Third-party widgets

```
useEffect(() => {
  const handler = () => {};
  window.addEventListener('resize', handler);
  return () => window.removeEventListener('resize', handler);
}, []);
```

- **Pros:** Mobile web highly optimized
- **Pros:** Strong sale-day runbooks
- **Cons:** Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
- **Cons:** MFE skew incidents during peaks

<a name="browser-requestanimationframe"></a>

### requestAnimationFrame

**Reasoning**

**Reasoning** Schedule callback before next repaint ~60Hz — smooth animations synced to display.

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**
Schedule callback before next repaint ~60Hz — smooth animations synced to display.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

**Example**

**Example**
```
function tick() { update(); requestAnimationFrame(tick); }
```

**Tradeoff**

**Trade-off** Better than setInterval for visual updates.

> **Tip:** Cancel with cancelAnimationFrame on unmount.

**Production**

*Context:* Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: requestAnimationFrame.

<a name="browser-requestidlecallback"></a>

### requestIdleCallback

**Reasoning**

**Reasoning** Run non-critical work when browser idle — analytics batch low priority.

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**
Run non-critical work when browser idle — analytics batch low priority.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

**Example**

**Example**
```
requestIdleCallback(() => sendAnalytics(), { timeout: 2000 })
```

**Tradeoff**

**Trade-off** Safari limited — setTimeout fallback.

> **Tip:** Don't starve if timeout fires.

**Production**

**Production example — Amazon**
*Context:* E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: requestIdleCallback.

*Why they chose this approach:* Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.
- **Pros:** Battle-tested idempotency patterns
- **Pros:** Massive A/B infra
- **Cons:** Legacy Webpack islands remain
- **Cons:** Many teams = inconsistent patterns

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **DevTools?** Name exact panel: Network waterfall, Performance trace, Application cache, Rendering paint flashing.
- **Core Web Vital?** Map answer to LCP (load), INP (input delay), CLS (layout shift) — pick the most relevant one.
- **Safari / WebView?** ITP cookie limits, missing APIs (e.g. some SW features), passive touch listeners — state what you polyfill or degrade.

**Probes**

**If the interviewer pushes deeper**- Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
- Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
- State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

<a name="browser-performanceobserver"></a>

### PerformanceObserver

**Reasoning**

**Reasoning** Subscribe to LCP, layout-shift, longtask entries — RUM collection.

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**
Subscribe to LCP, layout-shift, longtask entries — RUM collection.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

**Example**

**Example**
```
new PerformanceObserver(list => report(list.getEntries())).observe({ type: "largest-contentful-paint" })
```

**Tradeoff**

**Trade-off** Buffer size limits — handle drops.

> **Tip:** web-vitals library wraps this.

**Production**

**Production example — Flipkart**
*Context:* Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: PerformanceObserver.

*Why they chose this approach:* Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.
- **Pros:** Mobile web highly optimized
- **Pros:** Strong sale-day runbooks
- **Cons:** Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
- **Cons:** MFE skew incidents during peaks

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **DevTools?** Name exact panel: Network waterfall, Performance trace, Application cache, Rendering paint flashing.

<a name="browser-long-tasks-gt-50ms"></a>

### Long tasks >50ms

**Reasoning**

**Reasoning** Block main thread — delay input response hurting INP.

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**
Block main thread — delay input response hurting INP.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

**Example**

**Example**
```
PerformanceObserver longtask entries
```

**Tradeoff**

**Trade-off** Split task: scheduler.postTask or chunk loop.

> **Tip:** Chrome DevTools Performance flame chart.

**Production**

**Production example — Instagram**
*Context:* Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: Long tasks >50ms.

*Why they chose this approach:* Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.
- **Pros:** Feed scroll 60fps target
- **Pros:** Strong media CDN pipeline
- **Cons:** Hydration cost on web
- **Cons:** Moderation + ads complicate feed logic

**Reasoning**

**Example**

> **Tip:** Offload from hot path.

<a name="browser-bfcache-back-forward-cache"></a>

### bfcache back-forward cache

**Reasoning**

**Reasoning** Page snapshot in memory — instant back navigation.

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**
Page snapshot in memory — instant back navigation.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

**Example**

**Example**
```
pageshow event persisted property
```

**Tradeoff**

**Trade-off** unload listeners disable bfcache — use pagehide.

> **Tip:** See journeys section Q9.

**Production**

**Production example — Myntra**
*Context:* PWA SW caches app shell; product API network-first during End of Reason Sale.

*Why they chose this approach:* Versioned precache + runtime image cache with TTL.
- **Pros:** Repeat visit TTI faster
- **Pros:** Offline recently viewed PLP
- **Cons:** Stale price if cache-first on API
- **Cons:** SW update UX needed

<a name="browser-content-encoding-gzip-br"></a>

### Content-Encoding gzip br

**Reasoning**

**Reasoning** Brotli better compression than gzip — server negotiates Accept-Encoding.

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**
Brotli better compression than gzip — server negotiates Accept-Encoding.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

**Example**

**Example**
```
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate, br
```

**Tradeoff**

**Trade-off** Pre-compress assets at build for static host.

> **Tip:** Smaller JS faster download.

**Production**

**Production example — Amazon**
*Context:* E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Content-Encoding gzip br.

*Why they chose this approach:* Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.
- **Pros:** Battle-tested idempotency patterns
- **Pros:** Massive A/B infra
- **Cons:** Legacy Webpack islands remain
- **Cons:** Many teams = inconsistent patterns

## DOM &amp; Events (28 questions) High priority

<a name="dom-dom-tree-vs-shadow-dom-vs-virtual-dom"></a>

### DOM tree vs shadow DOM vs virtual DOM

- **DOM:** browser's live tree of nodes.
- **Shadow DOM:** encapsulated subtree — Web Components.
- **Virtual DOM:** React's JS representation — diffed then patched to real DOM.

**Production**

**Production example — Instagram**
*Context:* Feed uses virtualized list + IO sentinel for infinite scroll — constant DOM node count.

*Why they chose this approach:* react-window + Intersection Observer fetchNextPage.
- **Pros:** 10k posts scrollable without crash
- **Pros:** Prefetch next page before sentinel visible
- **Cons:** Focus management complex in virtual list
- **Cons:** Dynamic height rows hard to measure

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **React vs DOM?** React batches updates via Fiber, synthetic events, declarative diff — contrast with manual reflow from direct DOM writes.
- **A11y?** Semantic elements, ARIA only when needed, focus management on route/modal — tie DOM API to screen reader behavior.
- **Low-end perf?** Layout thrashing from read/write interleave, large DOM trees — mention virtualization or content-visibility.

**Probes**

**If the interviewer pushes deeper**- Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
- Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
- State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

---

<a name="dom-event-propagation-capture-target-bubble"></a>

### Event propagation — capture, target, bubble

```
parent.addEventListener('click', handler, true);  // capture phase
child.addEventListener('click', handler);         // bubble phase (default)
// Order: capture (root→target) → target → bubble (target→root)
```

`event.stopPropagation()` stops further propagation; `stopImmediatePropagation()` blocks other listeners on same node.

**Production**

**Production example — Amazon**
*Context:* E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Event propagation — capture, target, bubble..

*Why they chose this approach:* Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.
- **Pros:** Battle-tested idempotency patterns
- **Pros:** Massive A/B infra
- **Cons:** Legacy Webpack islands remain
- **Cons:** Many teams = inconsistent patterns

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **React vs DOM?** React batches updates via Fiber, synthetic events, declarative diff — contrast with manual reflow from direct DOM writes.
- **A11y?** Semantic elements, ARIA only when needed, focus management on route/modal — tie DOM API to screen reader behavior.
- **Low-end perf?** Layout thrashing from read/write interleave, large DOM trees — mention virtualization or content-visibility.

**Probes**

**If the interviewer pushes deeper**- Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
- Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
- State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

<a name="dom-event-delegation-pattern"></a>

### Event delegation pattern

```
document.querySelector('#list').addEventListener('click', (e) => {
  const row = e.target.closest('[data-id]');
  if (!row) return;
  handleRowClick(row.dataset.id);
});
```

One listener for dynamic lists — React uses delegation at root for synthetic events (legacy model).

**Production**

**Production example — Instagram**
*Context:* Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: Event delegation pattern..

*Why they chose this approach:* Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.
- **Pros:** Feed scroll 60fps target
- **Pros:** Strong media CDN pipeline
- **Cons:** Hydration cost on web
- **Cons:** Moderation + ads complicate feed logic

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **React vs DOM?** React batches updates via Fiber, synthetic events, declarative diff — contrast with manual reflow from direct DOM writes.
- **A11y?** Semantic elements, ARIA only when needed, focus management on route/modal — tie DOM API to screen reader behavior.
- **Low-end perf?** Layout thrashing from read/write interleave, large DOM trees — mention virtualization or content-visibility.

<a name="dom-passive-event-listeners-scroll-performance"></a>

### Passive event listeners — scroll performance

```
element.addEventListener('touchstart', handler, { passive: true });
// Browser won't wait for preventDefault — smoother scroll
```

Non-passive touch/wheel listeners can block scrolling — Lighthouse flags this.

**Production**

**Production example — Instagram**
*Context:* Feed uses virtualized list + IO sentinel for infinite scroll — constant DOM node count.

*Why they chose this approach:* react-window + Intersection Observer fetchNextPage.
- **Pros:** 10k posts scrollable without crash
- **Pros:** Prefetch next page before sentinel visible
- **Cons:** Focus management complex in virtual list
- **Cons:** Dynamic height rows hard to measure

<a name="dom-dom-manipulation-performance-documentfragment"></a>

### DOM manipulation performance — DocumentFragment

```
const frag = document.createDocumentFragment();
items.forEach(item => frag.appendChild(createRow(item)));
tbody.appendChild(frag); // single reflow
```

Batch DOM writes; avoid interleaved read/write (layout thrashing).

**Production**

**Production example — Instagram**
*Context:* Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: DOM manipulation performance — DocumentFragment..

*Why they chose this approach:* Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.
- **Pros:** Feed scroll 60fps target
- **Pros:** Strong media CDN pipeline
- **Cons:** Hydration cost on web
- **Cons:** Moderation + ads complicate feed logic

<a name="dom-layout-thrashing-read-write-interleaving"></a>

### Layout thrashing — read/write interleaving

```
// BAD — forces sync layout each iteration
els.forEach(el => { el.style.left = el.offsetWidth + 'px'; });
// GOOD — read all, then write all
const widths = els.map(el => el.offsetWidth);
els.forEach((el, i) => { el.style.left = widths[i] + 'px'; });
```

**Production**

**Production example — X (Twitter)**
*Context:* Realtime timeline — WebSocket push, optimistic compose, virtualized list for high event rate. Relevant when discussing: Layout thrashing — read/write interleaving..

*Why they chose this approach:* Batch DOM updates during viral events; reconnect with dedupe ids.
- **Pros:** Sub-second timeline updates
- **Pros:** Optimistic tweet post UX
- **Cons:** WS fragile on corporate networks
- **Cons:** Main-thread pressure during live events

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **React vs DOM?** React batches updates via Fiber, synthetic events, declarative diff — contrast with manual reflow from direct DOM writes.
- **A11y?** Semantic elements, ARIA only when needed, focus management on route/modal — tie DOM API to screen reader behavior.
- **Low-end perf?** Layout thrashing from read/write interleave, large DOM trees — mention virtualization or content-visibility.

**Probes**

**If the interviewer pushes deeper**- Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
- Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.

<a name="dom-customevent-for-decoupled-dom-communication"></a>

### CustomEvent for decoupled DOM communication

```
window.dispatchEvent(new CustomEvent('cart:updated', { detail: { count: 3 } }));
window.addEventListener('cart:updated', (e) => updateBadge(e.detail.count));
```

Useful for vanilla widgets; micro-frontends often use same pattern on shared bus.

<a name="dom-accessibility-tree-vs-dom-tree"></a>

### Accessibility tree vs DOM tree

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**1. Browser builds accessibility tree from DOM + ARIA — what screen readers use.

Browser builds accessibility tree from DOM + ARIA — what screen readers use. Hidden CSS (`display:none`) removes from a11y tree; `aria-hidden` explicitly hides decorative elements.

## JavaScript &amp; TypeScript (30 questions) High priority

<a name="js-event-loop-async-await-execution-order"></a>

### Event loop + async/await execution order

```
async function foo() {
  console.log('A');
  await bar();
  console.log('B'); // microtask after bar resolves
}
async function bar() { console.log('C'); }
foo(); console.log('D');
// A, C, D, B
```

**Production**

**Production example — X (Twitter)**
*Context:* Timeline merge batches incoming tweet promises — microtask queue floods if unbounded.

*Why they chose this approach:* requestAnimationFrame batch DOM inserts for 50+ new tweets/sec during live event.
- **Pros:** Main thread stays responsive
- **Pros:** No lost tweets on race
- **Cons:** Complex batching logic
- **Cons:** Debugging async order harder

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **TypeScript boundary?** Narrow types at API parse (Zod/io-ts), avoid any on event payloads, discriminated unions for state machines.
- **Junior bug?** Pick classic: closure in loop, mutable default args, floating promise without catch, == vs === on IDs.
- **Testing?** Unit for pure logic, RTL for DOM behavior, MSW for network — match test type to failure mode.

**Probes**

**If the interviewer pushes deeper**- Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.

<a name="js-closures-practical-use-and-memory-implications"></a>

### Closures — practical use and memory implications

```
function createCounter() {
  let count = 0;
  return () => ++count; // closes over count
}
```

Leaks when closure captures large scope unnecessarily — null out refs when done.

**Production**

**Production example — Netflix**
*Context:* Streaming UI prioritizes playback quality and browse LCP — perf budgets enforced per route. Relevant when discussing: Closures — practical use and memory implications..

- **Pros:** Edge CDN for static assets
- **Cons:** Personalization complicates caching
- **Cons:** DRM constraints on web player

<a name="js-typescript-structural-typing-vs-nominal"></a>

### TypeScript structural typing vs nominal

```
interface User { id: string; name: string; }
interface Admin { id: string; name: string; role: 'admin'; }
function greet(u: User) { return u.name; }
greet(admin); // OK if shape compatible — structural
```

Branded types for nominal simulation: `type UserId = string & { readonly brand: unique symbol }`.

<a name="js-generics-constrain-and-infer"></a>

### Generics — constrain and infer

```
function pick(obj: T, keys: K[]): Pick {
  const result = {} as Pick;
  keys.forEach(k => { result[k] = obj[k]; });
  return result;
}
```

<a name="js-discriminated-unions-for-api-results"></a>

### Discriminated unions for API results

```
type Result =
  | { ok: true; data: T }
  | { ok: false; error: string };

  if (r.ok) console.log(r.data.name); // narrowed
  else console.error(r.error);
}
```

<a name="js-promise-all-vs-allsettled-vs-race"></a>

### Promise.all vs allSettled vs race

- **all:** fail fast on first rejection
- **allSettled:** wait for all — partial success dashboards
- **race:** first settle wins — timeout pattern

**Production**

**Production example — X (Twitter)**
*Context:* Timeline merge batches incoming tweet promises — microtask queue floods if unbounded.

*Why they chose this approach:* requestAnimationFrame batch DOM inserts for 50+ new tweets/sec during live event.
- **Pros:** Main thread stays responsive
- **Pros:** No lost tweets on race
- **Cons:** Complex batching logic
- **Cons:** Debugging async order harder

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **TypeScript boundary?** Narrow types at API parse (Zod/io-ts), avoid any on event payloads, discriminated unions for state machines.

<a name="js-debounce-vs-throttle-implement-debounce"></a>

### Debounce vs throttle — implement debounce

```
function debounce(fn, ms) {
  let t;
  return (...args) => {
    clearTimeout(t);
    t = setTimeout(() => fn(...args), ms);
  };
}
```

**Production**

**Production example — Netflix**
*Context:* Streaming UI prioritizes playback quality and browse LCP — perf budgets enforced per route. Relevant when discussing: Debounce vs throttle — implement debounce..

## CSS vs SCSS vs Tailwind (24 questions) High priority

<a name="styling-compare-css-scss-and-tailwind-when-to-choose-each"></a>

### Compare CSS, SCSS, and Tailwind — when to choose each

ApproachProsCons**Plain CSS**Zero tooling, native vars, cascade layersScale needs discipline (BEM)**SCSS**Variables, nesting, mixins, partialsBuild step; nesting abuse**Tailwind**Speed, design tokens, purge unusedVerbose JSX, learning curve
> **Tip:** Trend: CSS native features (nesting, vars) reduce SCSS need; Tailwind dominates greenfield startups.

**Production**

**Production example — Myntra**
*Context:* Internal tools built with Tailwind + design tokens; consumer site hybrid CSS modules + tokens.

*Why they chose this approach:* Tailwind for speed on admin; tokens from Style Dictionary for brand consistency.
- **Pros:** Fast iteration on seller portal
- **Pros:** Purge removes unused utilities in prod
- **Cons:** HTML noise in consumer components
- **Cons:** Migration from legacy SCSS ongoing

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **Greenfield 2025?** Tailwind or CSS modules + tokens; avoid global SCSS soup unless team mandate; co-locate styles with components.
- **SCSS migration?** Strangler: new components CSS modules, token map from $variables to custom properties, delete unused partials incrementally.
- **Bundle impact?** Purge/Treeshake Tailwind; critical CSS for above-fold; measure computed stylesheet size in Coverage tab.

**Probes**

**If the interviewer pushes deeper**- Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
- Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
- State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

---

<a name="styling-scss-nesting-best-practices-and-pitfalls"></a>

### SCSS nesting — best practices and pitfalls

```
// GOOD — BEM with shallow nesting
.card {
  &__title { font-size: 1.25rem; }
  &--featured { border-color: blue; }
}
// BAD — mirrors DOM depth, specificity explosion
.page .sidebar .nav .item a { color: red; }
```

**Production**

**Production example — Amazon**
*Context:* Legacy Seller Central SCSS partials migrating to CSS modules per route strangler.

*Why they chose this approach:* Co-locate module per component; token map from SCSS vars to CSS vars.
- **Pros:** No global cascade bugs on new code
- **Pros:** Incremental migration
- **Cons:** Dual styling systems during transition
- **Cons:** Developer confusion which pattern to use

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **Greenfield 2025?** Tailwind or CSS modules + tokens; avoid global SCSS soup unless team mandate; co-locate styles with components.
- **SCSS migration?** Strangler: new components CSS modules, token map from $variables to custom properties, delete unused partials incrementally.
- **Bundle impact?** Purge/Treeshake Tailwind; critical CSS for above-fold; measure computed stylesheet size in Coverage tab.

**Probes**

**If the interviewer pushes deeper**- Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
- Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
- State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

---

<a name="styling-scss-mixins-vs-css-layer-modern-alternative"></a>

### SCSS mixins vs CSS @layer — modern alternative

```
@mixin focus-ring { outline: 2px solid var(--focus); }
// CSS today:
@layer utilities {
  .focus-ring:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid var(--focus); }
}
```

Mixins still useful for complex parameterized patterns; layers control cascade order natively.

**Production**

**Production example — Amazon**
*Context:* Legacy Seller Central SCSS partials migrating to CSS modules per route strangler.

*Why they chose this approach:* Co-locate module per component; token map from SCSS vars to CSS vars.
- **Pros:** No global cascade bugs on new code
- **Pros:** Incremental migration
- **Cons:** Dual styling systems during transition
- **Cons:** Developer confusion which pattern to use

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **Greenfield 2025?** Tailwind or CSS modules + tokens; avoid global SCSS soup unless team mandate; co-locate styles with components.
- **SCSS migration?** Strangler: new components CSS modules, token map from $variables to custom properties, delete unused partials incrementally.
- **Bundle impact?** Purge/Treeshake Tailwind; critical CSS for above-fold; measure computed stylesheet size in Coverage tab.

**Probes**

**If the interviewer pushes deeper**- Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
- Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
- State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

<a name="styling-tailwind-apply-when-acceptable"></a>

### Tailwind @apply — when acceptable

```
@layer components {
  .btn-primary {
    @apply inline-flex items-center rounded-md bg-blue-600 px-4 py-2 text-white hover:bg-blue-700;
  }
}
```

OK for design-system primitives. Overuse duplicates Tailwind benefits — prefer utilities in markup for one-offs.

**Production**

**Production example — Myntra**
*Context:* Internal tools built with Tailwind + design tokens; consumer site hybrid CSS modules + tokens.

*Why they chose this approach:* Tailwind for speed on admin; tokens from Style Dictionary for brand consistency.

<a name="styling-tailwind-jit-and-purging-how-bundle-stays-small"></a>

### Tailwind JIT and purging — how bundle stays small

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**1. Scans source files for class strings; generates only used utilities.
2. Dynamic class names break purge:

*Pro move:* tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Scans source files for class strings; generates only used utilities. Dynamic class names break purge:

```
// BAD — purge can't see full string
const color = 'red'; className={`text-${color}-500`}
// GOOD
const map = { red: 'text-red-500', blue: 'text-blue-500' };
```

**Production**

**Production example — Myntra**
*Context:* Internal tools built with Tailwind + design tokens; consumer site hybrid CSS modules + tokens.

*Why they chose this approach:* Tailwind for speed on admin; tokens from Style Dictionary for brand consistency.
- **Pros:** Fast iteration on seller portal
- **Pros:** Purge removes unused utilities in prod
- **Cons:** HTML noise in consumer components
- **Cons:** Migration from legacy SCSS ongoing

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **Greenfield 2025?** Tailwind or CSS modules + tokens; avoid global SCSS soup unless team mandate; co-locate styles with components.
- **SCSS migration?** Strangler: new components CSS modules, token map from $variables to custom properties, delete unused partials incrementally.
- **Bundle impact?** Purge/Treeshake Tailwind; critical CSS for above-fold; measure computed stylesheet size in Coverage tab.

**Probes**

**If the interviewer pushes deeper**- Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
- Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
- State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

---

<a name="styling-design-tokens-implement-across-css-scss-tailwind"></a>

### Design tokens — implement across CSS/SCSS/Tailwind

```
/* CSS vars — source of truth */
:root { --space-4: 1rem; --color-brand: #2563eb; }
/* tailwind.config theme.extend */
colors: { brand: 'var(--color-brand)' }
```

Single token source synced to Tailwind theme and SCSS variables via Style Dictionary.

**Production**

**Production example — Myntra**
*Context:* Internal tools built with Tailwind + design tokens; consumer site hybrid CSS modules + tokens.

*Why they chose this approach:* Tailwind for speed on admin; tokens from Style Dictionary for brand consistency.
- **Pros:** Fast iteration on seller portal
- **Pros:** Purge removes unused utilities in prod
- **Cons:** HTML noise in consumer components
- **Cons:** Migration from legacy SCSS ongoing

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **Greenfield 2025?** Tailwind or CSS modules + tokens; avoid global SCSS soup unless team mandate; co-locate styles with components.
- **SCSS migration?** Strangler: new components CSS modules, token map from $variables to custom properties, delete unused partials incrementally.
- **Bundle impact?** Purge/Treeshake Tailwind; critical CSS for above-fold; measure computed stylesheet size in Coverage tab.

**Probes**

**If the interviewer pushes deeper**- Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
- Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
- State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

<a name="styling-css-modules-vs-tailwind-in-react"></a>

### CSS Modules vs Tailwind in React

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**
Modules: scoped class names, colocated Button.module.css , zero runtime.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

**Modules:** scoped class names, colocated `Button.module.css`, zero runtime.

**Tailwind:** faster iteration, consistent spacing scale, worse for highly dynamic arbitrary styles.

Hybrid common: Tailwind layout + CSS modules for complex animations.

**Production**

**Production example — Myntra**
*Context:* Internal tools built with Tailwind + design tokens; consumer site hybrid CSS modules + tokens.

*Why they chose this approach:* Tailwind for speed on admin; tokens from Style Dictionary for brand consistency.
- **Pros:** Fast iteration on seller portal
- **Pros:** Purge removes unused utilities in prod
- **Cons:** HTML noise in consumer components
- **Cons:** Migration from legacy SCSS ongoing

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **Greenfield 2025?** Tailwind or CSS modules + tokens; avoid global SCSS soup unless team mandate; co-locate styles with components.
- **SCSS migration?** Strangler: new components CSS modules, token map from $variables to custom properties, delete unused partials incrementally.
- **Bundle impact?** Purge/Treeshake Tailwind; critical CSS for above-fold; measure computed stylesheet size in Coverage tab.

**Probes**

**If the interviewer pushes deeper**- Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
- Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
- State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

<a name="styling-scss-use-vs-deprecated-import"></a>

### SCSS @use vs deprecated @import

```
@use 'tokens/colors' as c;
.button { background: c.$primary; }
```

@use loads once, namespaced — fixes duplicate CSS and global pollution from @import.

**Production**

**Production example — Amazon**
*Context:* Legacy Seller Central SCSS partials migrating to CSS modules per route strangler.

*Why they chose this approach:* Co-locate module per component; token map from SCSS vars to CSS vars.
- **Pros:** No global cascade bugs on new code
- **Pros:** Incremental migration
- **Cons:** Dual styling systems during transition
- **Cons:** Developer confusion which pattern to use

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **Greenfield 2025?** Tailwind or CSS modules + tokens; avoid global SCSS soup unless team mandate; co-locate styles with components.
- **SCSS migration?** Strangler: new components CSS modules, token map from $variables to custom properties, delete unused partials incrementally.
- **Bundle impact?** Purge/Treeshake Tailwind; critical CSS for above-fold; measure computed stylesheet size in Coverage tab.

**Probes**

**If the interviewer pushes deeper**- Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
- Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
- State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

<a name="styling-css-in-js-vs-tailwind-in-2025-senior-take"></a>

### CSS-in-JS vs Tailwind in 2025 — senior take

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**1. CSS-in-JS (styled-components) loses favor: runtime cost, RSC incompatibility, streaming issues.
2. Tailwind + CSS modules + vanilla-extract (zero runtime) preferred for new apps.
3. Know trade-offs, not tribalism.

*Pro move:* tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

CSS-in-JS (styled-components) loses favor: runtime cost, RSC incompatibility, streaming issues. Tailwind + CSS modules + vanilla-extract (zero runtime) preferred for new apps. Know trade-offs, not tribalism.

**Production**

**Production example — Myntra**
*Context:* Internal tools built with Tailwind + design tokens; consumer site hybrid CSS modules + tokens.

*Why they chose this approach:* Tailwind for speed on admin; tokens from Style Dictionary for brand consistency.
- **Pros:** Fast iteration on seller portal
- **Pros:** Purge removes unused utilities in prod
- **Cons:** HTML noise in consumer components
- **Cons:** Migration from legacy SCSS ongoing

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **Greenfield 2025?** Tailwind or CSS modules + tokens; avoid global SCSS soup unless team mandate; co-locate styles with components.
- **SCSS migration?** Strangler: new components CSS modules, token map from $variables to custom properties, delete unused partials incrementally.
- **Bundle impact?** Purge/Treeshake Tailwind; critical CSS for above-fold; measure computed stylesheet size in Coverage tab.

**Probes**

**If the interviewer pushes deeper**- Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
- Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
- State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

<a name="styling-bem-naming-convention"></a>

### BEM naming convention

**Reasoning**

**Reasoning** Block__element--modifier avoids specificity wars plain CSS.

**Deep**

**Interview talking points (expand aloud)**
Block__element--modifier avoids specificity wars plain CSS.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

**Example**

**Example**
```
.card__title--featured
```

**Tradeoff**

**Trade-off** Verbose class names — Tailwind avoids differently.

> **Tip:** Legacy codebase common.

**Production**

**Production example — Flipkart**
*Context:* Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: BEM naming convention.

*Why they chose this approach:* Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.
- **Pros:** Mobile web highly optimized
- **Pros:** Strong sale-day runbooks
- **Cons:** Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
- **Cons:** MFE skew incidents during peaks

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **Greenfield 2025?** Tailwind or CSS modules + tokens; avoid global SCSS soup unless team mandate; co-locate styles with components.
- **SCSS migration?** Strangler: new components CSS modules, token map from $variables to custom properties, delete unused partials incrementally.
- **Bundle impact?** Purge/Treeshake Tailwind; critical CSS for above-fold; measure computed stylesheet size in Coverage tab.

**Example**

- **Pros:** Design system for fashion components
- **Cons:** Huge image bytes vs general ecommerce
- **Cons:** Filter state in URL complexity

**Production**

**Production example — Flipkart**
*Context:* Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: Stylelint enforcement.

*Why they chose this approach:* Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.
- **Pros:** Mobile web highly optimized
- **Pros:** Strong sale-day runbooks
- **Cons:** Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
- **Cons:** MFE skew incidents during peaks

**Followup**

**Follow-up Q&A (with answers)**- **Greenfield 2025?** Tailwind or CSS modules + tokens; avoid global SCSS soup unless team mandate; co-locate styles with components.
- **SCSS migration?** Strangler: new components CSS modules, token map from $variables to custom properties, delete unused partials incrementally.
- **Bundle impact?** Purge/Treeshake Tailwind; critical CSS for above-fold; measure computed stylesheet size in Coverage tab.
